title: Longest Duplicate Substring
description: Return any longest substring that appears at least twice. Example: banana -> ana

examples.explanation:
1. Initialize longest = ''
2. i=0: j=1: substring='b', check duplicates: none found
   j=2: 'ba', no duplicates
   j=3: 'ban', no duplicates
   j=4: 'bana', no duplicates
   j=5: 'banan', no duplicates
   j=6: 'banana', no duplicates
3. i=1: j=2: 'a', duplicates at positions 3,5 → length=1 > longest length=0 → longest='a'
   j=3: 'an', duplicates at position 3 → length=2 > 1 → longest='an'
   j=4: 'ana', duplicates at position 3 → length=3 > 2 → longest='ana'
   j=5: 'anan', no duplicates
   j=6: 'anana', no duplicates
4. i=2: j=3: 'n', duplicates at position 4 → length=1 < 3
   j=4: 'na', duplicates at position 4 → length=2 < 3
   j=5: 'nan', no duplicates
   j=6: 'nana', no duplicates
5. i=3: j=4: 'a', duplicates at positions 1,5 → length=1 < 3, no update
   j=5: 'an', duplicate at position 1 (s[1:3]='an') → length=2 < 3, no update
   j=6: 'ana', duplicate at position 1 (s[1:4]='ana') → length=3, not > 3, no update
6. i=4: j=5: 'n', duplicates at position 2 → length=1 < 3
   j=6: 'na', no duplicates
7. i=5: j=6: 'a', duplicates at positions 1,3 → length=1 < 3
8. Return longest='ana'

1. Initialize longest = ''
2. i=0: j=1: 'a', no duplicates
   j=2: 'ab', no duplicates
   j=3: 'abc', no duplicates
   j=4: 'abcd', no duplicates
3. i=1: j=2: 'b', no duplicates
   j=3: 'bc', no duplicates
   j=4: 'bcd', no duplicates
4. i=2: j=3: 'c', no duplicates
   j=4: 'cd', no duplicates
5. i=3: j=4: 'd', no duplicates
6. Return longest='' (empty string)

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n^3)
approaches.summary: Generate all possible substrings and check for duplicates using nested loops. This is straightforward but inefficient, suitable only for small strings due to its cubic time complexity.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Longest Duplicate Substring
ladders.desc: Complete O(n³) solution generating all substrings and checking for duplicates.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to find the longest substring that appears at least twice in a given string. The intuition is straightforward: if we examine every possible substring and check if it appears elsewhere in the string, we're guaranteed to find the longest duplicate substring. The algorithm works by generating all substrings through nested loops for start and end indices, then for each substring, scanning the rest of the string to find a duplicate occurrence. We maintain the longest duplicate found so far. The time complexity is O(n³) because we have O(n²) substrings, and checking each substring takes O(n) time in worst case. Space complexity is O(n²) to store all substrings in worst case. This connects to L1 by using the duplicate checking subroutine as its core component. Common mistakes include: 1) Not handling empty string or no duplicate cases, 2) Off-by-one errors in substring indices, 3) Inefficient duplicate checking that doesn't break early, 4) Case sensitivity issues. The real pattern: initialize longest = '', iterate i from 0 to n-1, iterate j from i+1 to n, get substring s[i:j], check if it appears elsewhere in s, if yes and length > longest length, update longest. Return longest.

examples.trace:
1. Initialize longest = ''
2. i=0: j=1: substring='b', check duplicates: none found
   j=2: 'ba', no duplicates
   j=3: 'ban', no duplicates
   j=4: 'bana', no duplicates
   j=5: 'banan', no duplicates
   j=6: 'banana', no duplicates
3. i=1: j=2: 'a', duplicates at positions 3,5 → length=1 > longest length=0 → longest='a'
   j=3: 'an', duplicates at position 3 → length=2 > 1 → longest='an'
   j=4: 'ana', duplicates at position 3 → length=3 > 2 → longest='ana'
   j=5: 'anan', no duplicates
   j=6: 'anana', no duplicates
4. i=2: j=3: 'n', duplicates at position 4 → length=1 < 3
   j=4: 'na', duplicates at position 4 → length=2 < 3
   j=5: 'nan', no duplicates
   j=6: 'nana', no duplicates
5. i=3: j=4: 'a', duplicates at positions 1,5 → length=1 < 3, no update
   j=5: 'an', duplicate at position 1 (s[1:3]='an') → length=2 < 3, no update
   j=6: 'ana', duplicate at position 1 (s[1:4]='ana') → length=3, not > 3, no update
6. i=4: j=5: 'n', duplicates at position 2 → length=1 < 3
   j=6: 'na', no duplicates
7. i=5: j=6: 'a', duplicates at positions 1,3 → length=1 < 3
8. Return longest='ana'

1. Initialize longest = ''
2. i=0: j=1: 'a', no duplicates
   j=2: 'ab', no duplicates
   j=3: 'abc', no duplicates
   j=4: 'abcd', no duplicates
3. i=1: j=2: 'b', no duplicates
   j=3: 'bc', no duplicates
   j=4: 'bcd', no duplicates
4. i=2: j=3: 'c', no duplicates
   j=4: 'cd', no duplicates
5. i=3: j=4: 'd', no duplicates
6. Return longest='' (empty string)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Check for Duplicate Substring
ladders.desc: Function to check if a given substring appears elsewhere in the string.
ladders.explanation: This is the most critical helper function in the brute force approach. The intuition is simple: given a substring and the original string, we need to determine if that exact sequence of characters appears at least one more time in the string (not counting the original occurrence). The algorithm slides through the string, comparing the target substring with every possible substring of equal length. We start from the beginning of the string and move to position n-len(substring), extracting substrings of matching length and comparing character by character. The time complexity is O(n*m) where n is string length and m is substring length, but in worst case m≈n giving O(n²). Space complexity is O(1) aside from the input storage. This feeds into L0 by being called for every generated substring. Common mistakes: 1) Not handling the case where substring appears at the same starting position (should be skipped), 2) Not breaking early when mismatch found, 3) Off-by-one errors in loop bounds. Real pattern: function has_duplicate(s, substr, start_idx): n=len(s), m=len(substr); for i in range(0, n-m+1): if i == start_idx: continue; match=True; for j in range(m): if s[i+j] != substr[j]: match=False; break; if match: return True; return False.

examples.trace:
1. n=6, m=3
2. i=0: i≠1, compare s[0:3]='ban' with 'ana': first char 'b'≠'a' → mismatch
3. i=1: i=1 → skip (original position)
4. i=2: compare s[2:5]='nan' with 'ana': 'n'≠'a' → mismatch
5. i=3: compare s[3:6]='ana' with 'ana': chars match: 'a'='a', 'n'='n', 'a'='a' → return True

1. n=5, m=2
2. i=0: compare s[0:2]='he' with 'll': 'h'≠'l' → mismatch
3. i=1: compare s[1:3]='el' with 'll': 'e'≠'l' → mismatch
4. i=2: i=2 → skip
5. i=3: compare s[3:5]='lo' with 'll': 'l'='l', 'o'≠'l' → mismatch
6. No matches found → return False

ladders.title: Core Logic — Generate All Substrings
ladders.desc: Algorithm to generate every possible substring of a string.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique in the brute force approach: systematically generating all possible contiguous subsequences of a string. The intuition is that any duplicate substring must be one of these generated substrings. We use two nested loops: the outer loop fixes the starting index i, and the inner loop varies the ending index j from i+1 to n (where n is string length). This produces substrings s[i:j] (from i inclusive to j exclusive). The algorithm generates n*(n+1)/2 substrings total, which is O(n²). Time complexity is O(n³) when combined with duplicate checking, but the generation itself is O(n²). Space complexity depends on whether we store all substrings (O(n³) characters total) or process them one by one (O(n) for current substring). This feeds into L1 by providing the substrings to check for duplicates. Common mistakes: 1) Using j<=n instead of j<=n (off-by-one), 2) Not understanding that j is exclusive in slicing, 3) Generating duplicate substrings unnecessarily. Real pattern: n=len(s); for i in range(n): for j in range(i+1, n+1): substr = s[i:j]; process substr.

examples.trace:
1. n=2
2. i=0: j=1 → s[0:1]='x'
   j=2 → s[0:2]='xy'
3. i=1: j=2 → s[1:2]='y'
4. Total 3 substrings generated

ladders.title: Building Block — String Slicing and Comparison
ladders.desc: Extracting substrings and comparing them character by character.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves two fundamental string operations: extracting a substring (slicing) and comparing two strings for equality. The intuition is that to check if a substring appears elsewhere, we need to extract candidate substrings from the original string and compare them with our target. String slicing creates a new string from a contiguous portion of the original, specified by start and end indices. String comparison checks if two strings have exactly the same characters in the same order. Time complexity for slicing is O(k) where k is slice length, as it copies characters. Comparison is also O(k) in worst case. Space complexity for slicing is O(k) for the new string. This feeds into L2 by being the mechanism to generate and compare substrings. Common mistakes: 1) Confusing inclusive/exclusive bounds in slicing, 2) Assuming string comparison is O(1), 3) Not handling empty slices correctly. Real pattern: substr = s[start:end] (end exclusive); comparison: substr1 == substr2 does character-by-character check.

examples.trace:
1. s='hello' has indices: 0:h, 1:e, 2:l, 3:l, 4:o
2. Slice s[1:4] takes characters from index 1 up to but not including index 4
3. Extract characters: index1='e', index2='l', index3='l'
4. Result: 'ell'

1. Compare character by character:
2. Position 0: 'a' vs 'b' → different
3. Since first characters differ, strings are not equal
4. Return False (don't need to check remaining characters)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate Through String Indices
ladders.desc: Looping through all valid positions in a string.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves using loops to access every character or position in a string. The intuition is that to examine substrings, we need to systematically visit all possible starting and ending positions. We use index-based iteration, typically with for loops from 0 to n-1 (where n is string length). This allows us to access individual characters via s[i] and to define substring ranges. Time complexity is O(n) for a single loop, O(n²) for nested loops. Space complexity is O(1) for the iteration itself. This feeds into L3 by providing the mechanism to traverse the string for slicing operations. Common mistakes: 1) Off-by-one errors (using <= n instead of < n), 2) Modifying string while iterating, 3) Confusing character indices with positions. Real pattern: n = len(s); for i in range(n): access s[i]; for nested: for i in range(n): for j in range(i+1, n+1): define substring s[i:j].

examples.trace:
1. n = len('cat') = 3
2. Loop i from 0 to 2:
   i=0: character s[0]='c'
   i=1: character s[1]='a'
   i=2: character s[2]='t'
3. All indices visited: 0,1,2

1. n=2
2. Outer i=0: inner j from 1 to 2: (0,1), (0,2)
3. Outer i=1: inner j from 2 to 2: (1,2)
4. Total index pairs: (0,1), (0,2), (1,2)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a String?
ladders.desc: Understanding strings as sequences of characters with indices.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding what a string is in programming. A string is an ordered sequence of characters, where each character has a position called an index (starting from 0). For example, in string 'hello', index 0 is 'h', index 1 is 'e', etc. The length of a string is the number of characters it contains. Strings are immutable in many languages (cannot be changed once created). This foundational concept feeds into L4 because to iterate through a string, you need to understand its indexed structure. Common beginner mistakes: 1) Thinking indices start at 1, 2) Confusing string length with last index (length-1), 3) Not understanding that spaces are characters too. Real pattern: s = 'example'; len(s) gives length 7; s[0] is 'e'; s[6] is 'e' (last character).

examples.trace:
1. String 'dog' has 3 characters
2. Index 0: character 'd'
3. Index 1: character 'o'
4. Index 2: character 'g'
5. Last index is 2 (length-1)

1. String 'a b' has 3 characters (including space)
2. Index 0: 'a'
3. Index 1: space character ' '
4. Index 2: 'b'
5. The space is a valid character at index 1

approaches.name: Binary Search with Rolling Hash O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Use binary search on the length of the substring and rolling hash (e.g., Rabin-Karp) to efficiently check for duplicate substrings of a given length. This reduces the problem to searching for duplicates in O(n) time per length check.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Duplicate Substring with Binary Search and Rolling Hash
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of the O(n log n) solution using binary search on substring length and rolling hash for duplicate detection.
ladders.explanation:
This solution combines binary search and rolling hash to efficiently find the longest duplicate substring. The intuition is that if a duplicate substring of length L exists, then duplicates of shorter lengths must also exist, allowing binary search on length. For each candidate length L, we compute rolling hashes of all substrings of length L in O(n) time using the Rabin-Karp algorithm. By storing hashes in a hash map, we can detect duplicates in O(1) average time. The binary search reduces the number of length checks from O(n) to O(log n), giving overall O(n log n) time complexity.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize low = 1, high = n-1 (maximum possible duplicate length)
2. While low ≤ high:
   a. mid = (low + high) // 2
   b. Check if duplicate substring of length mid exists using rolling hash
   c. If yes: store the substring, low = mid + 1
   d. If no: high = mid - 1
3. Return the stored substring

Rolling hash check for length L:
1. Compute base^L modulo MOD for efficient hash updates
2. Initialize hash for first L characters
3. Store hash in dictionary with starting index
4. Slide window through string:
   a. Remove leftmost character contribution
   b. Add new character contribution
   c. Update hash
   d. If hash exists in dictionary: return substring
   e. Else store hash with current index

Time complexity: O(n log n) - binary search O(log n) iterations, each O(n) for hash computation
Space complexity: O(n) - storing hashes and substrings

Connection to L1: This level integrates the duplicate detection subroutine from L1 with binary search framework.

Common mistakes:
1. Hash collisions: Use large prime modulus and optionally double hashing
2. Off-by-one errors in binary search bounds
3. Not handling empty string case
4. Forgetting to store actual substring when hash matches

Real code pattern:
def longestDupSubstring(s):
    n = len(s)
    MOD = 10**9+7
    BASE = 26
    
    def has_duplicate(L):
        # Rolling hash implementation
        power = pow(BASE, L, MOD)
        current_hash = 0
        for i in range(L):
            current_hash = (current_hash * BASE + ord(s[i])) % MOD
        
        seen = {current_hash: 0}
        for i in range(L, n):
            current_hash = (current_hash * BASE - ord(s[i-L]) * power + ord(s[i])) % MOD
            if current_hash in seen:
                return s[seen[current_hash]:seen[current_hash]+L]
            seen[current_hash] = i-L+1
        return ""
    
    low, high = 1, n-1
    result = ""
    while low <= high:
        mid = (low + high) // 2
        dup = has_duplicate(mid)
        if dup:
            result = dup
            low = mid + 1
        else:
            high = mid - 1
    return result

examples.trace:
Step 1: Binary search initialization: low=1, high=5
Step 2: Check mid=3:
  Compute rolling hashes for length 3:
  "ban" hash = h1
  "ana" hash = h2
  "nan" hash = h3
  "ana" hash = h2 (duplicate found!)
  Return "ana"
Step 3: Since duplicate found for length 3, search longer: low=4, high=5
Step 4: Check mid=4:
  Compute rolling hashes for length 4:
  "bana" hash = h4
  "anan" hash = h5
  "nana" hash = h6
  No duplicates found
Step 5: Search shorter: low=4, high=3 (binary search ends)
Step 6: Return "ana" (stored from length 3 check)

Step 1: Binary search initialization: low=1, high=3
Step 2: Check mid=2:
  Compute rolling hashes for length 2:
  "ab" hash = h1
  "bc" hash = h2
  "cd" hash = h3
  No duplicates found
Step 3: Search shorter: low=1, high=1
Step 4: Check mid=1:
  Compute rolling hashes for length 1:
  "a" hash = h4
  "b" hash = h5
  "c" hash = h6
  "d" hash = h7
  No duplicates found
Step 5: Search shorter: low=1, high=0 (binary search ends)
Step 6: Return "" (no duplicate found)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Rolling Hash Duplicate Detection for Fixed Length
ladders.desc: Check if any substring of given length L appears at least twice using rolling hash.
ladders.explanation:
This subroutine efficiently detects duplicate substrings of a fixed length L using the Rabin-Karp rolling hash algorithm. The intuition is that by computing polynomial hashes of substrings, we can compare substrings in O(1) time after O(n) preprocessing. The rolling hash allows us to compute each subsequent hash from the previous one in O(1) time by removing the contribution of the exiting character and adding the new character.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Precompute BASE^L modulo MOD for efficient removal of old characters
2. Compute hash of first L characters
3. Store this hash in a dictionary with starting index 0
4. For i from L to n-1:
   a. Remove contribution of s[i-L]: hash = (hash - ord(s[i-L]) * power) % MOD
   b. Add contribution of s[i]: hash = (hash * BASE + ord(s[i])) % MOD
   c. If hash exists in dictionary: return substring s[dictionary[hash]:dictionary[hash]+L]
   d. Else store hash with starting index i-L+1
5. Return empty string if no duplicate found

Time complexity: O(n) - single pass through the string
Space complexity: O(n) in worst case for storing all hashes

Connection to L0: This is the core duplicate detection function called during binary search.

Common mistakes:
1. Not handling negative hash values after subtraction
2. Forgetting to multiply by BASE when adding new character
3. Hash collisions causing false positives
4. Not storing the actual substring when hash matches

Real code pattern:
def has_duplicate_fixed_length(s, L):
    n = len(s)
    MOD = 10**9+7
    BASE = 26
    power = pow(BASE, L, MOD)
    
    current_hash = 0
    for i in range(L):
        current_hash = (current_hash * BASE + ord(s[i])) % MOD
    
    seen = {current_hash: 0}
    for i in range(L, n):
        current_hash = (current_hash * BASE - ord(s[i-L]) * power + ord(s[i])) % MOD
        if current_hash in seen:
            return s[seen[current_hash]:seen[current_hash]+L]
        seen[current_hash] = i-L+1
    return ""

examples.trace:
Step 1: Compute power = 26^3 mod MOD
Step 2: Initial hash for "ban":
  h = ((0*26 + 98)*26 + 97)*26 + 110 = h1
  Store h1 → index 0
Step 3: Slide to "ana":
  Remove 'b': h1 - 98*power
  Add 'a': (result*26 + 97) = h2
  h2 not in seen, store h2 → index 1
Step 4: Slide to "nan":
  Remove 'a': h2 - 97*power
  Add 'n': (result*26 + 110) = h3
  h3 not in seen, store h3 → index 2
Step 5: Slide to "ana":
  Remove 'n': h3 - 110*power
  Add 'a': (result*26 + 97) = h2
  h2 in seen at index 1!
Step 6: Return s[1:4] = "ana"

Step 1: Compute power = 26^2 mod MOD
Step 2: Initial hash for "ab":
  h = (0*26 + 97)*26 + 98 = h1
  Store h1 → index 0
Step 3: Slide to "bc":
  Remove 'a': h1 - 97*power
  Add 'c': (result*26 + 99) = h2
  h2 not in seen, store h2 → index 1
Step 4: Slide to "cd":
  Remove 'b': h2 - 98*power
  Add 'd': (result*26 + 100) = h3
  h3 not in seen, store h3 → index 2
Step 5: No duplicates found, return ""

ladders.title: Core Logic — Binary Search on Answer with Monotonic Property
ladders.desc: Use binary search to find maximum length L where duplicate substring exists.
ladders.explanation:
The core insight is that the property "has duplicate substring of length L" is monotonic: if a duplicate substring of length L exists, then duplicates of all shorter lengths must also exist (by taking prefixes). Conversely, if no duplicate of length L exists, then no duplicates of longer lengths exist. This monotonic property allows binary search on the answer length.

Intuition: Consider lengths from 1 to n-1. The function f(L) = "has duplicate of length L" is true for small L and false for large L (or vice versa). The transition point where f(L) changes from true to false gives us the maximum L with duplicates.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Initialize low = 1, high = n-1
2. While low ≤ high:
   a. mid = (low + high) // 2
   b. Check if duplicate substring of length mid exists
   c. If yes: store result, search longer lengths (low = mid + 1)
   d. If no: search shorter lengths (high = mid - 1)
3. Return stored result

Time complexity: O(log n) iterations
Space complexity: O(1) for binary search variables

Connection to L1: Binary search calls the duplicate detection subroutine at each step.

Common mistakes:
1. Incorrect binary search bounds (off-by-one)
2. Not storing the actual substring when found
3. Infinite loop with wrong termination condition
4. Assuming property holds without verification

Real code pattern:
def binary_search_duplicate(s):
    n = len(s)
    low, high = 1, n-1
    result = ""
    
    while low <= high:
        mid = (low + high) // 2
        # Call has_duplicate_fixed_length(s, mid)
        dup = has_duplicate(s, mid)
        
        if dup:  # Duplicate exists for length mid
            result = dup  # Store the actual substring
            low = mid + 1  # Search for longer duplicates
        else:
            high = mid - 1  # Search for shorter duplicates
    
    return result

examples.trace:
Step 1: n=6, low=1, high=5
Step 2: mid=3, check length 3: duplicate "ana" found
  Store "ana", low=4
Step 3: mid=4, check length 4: no duplicate found
  high=3
Step 4: low=4 > high=3, loop ends
Step 5: Return "ana"

Step 1: n=4, low=1, high=3
Step 2: mid=2, check length 2: no duplicate found
  high=1
Step 3: mid=1, check length 1: no duplicate found
  high=0
Step 4: low=1 > high=0, loop ends
Step 5: Return "" (no result stored)

ladders.title: Building Block — Rabin-Karp Rolling Hash Computation
ladders.desc: Compute polynomial hash of sliding window substrings in O(1) time per slide.
ladders.explanation:
The Rabin-Karp algorithm computes hashes of substrings using a rolling polynomial hash. The key insight is that the hash of a substring can be updated in O(1) time when sliding the window by one character, rather than recomputing from scratch in O(L) time.

Intuition: Represent a substring as a polynomial in base B: H(s[i:j]) = s[i]*B^(L-1) + s[i+1]*B^(L-2) + ... + s[j]*B^0 mod M. When sliding from position i to i+1, we remove s[i]*B^(L-1) and add s[i+L]*B^0, then multiply everything by B to shift positions.

Step-by-step update:
1. Precompute B^L mod M (call this power)
2. Initial hash: H0 = Σ s[k]*B^(L-1-k) for k=0 to L-1
3. To slide window: H_new = (H_old * B - s[outgoing]*power + s[incoming]) mod M

Time complexity: O(1) per slide after O(L) initialization
Space complexity: O(1)

Connection to L2: This efficient hash computation enables O(n) duplicate detection for fixed length.

Common mistakes:
1. Incorrect formula for hash update
2. Not handling negative values after subtraction
3. Integer overflow without modulo operations
4. Wrong base or modulus choices

Real code pattern:
def rolling_hash(s, L):
    MOD = 10**9+7
    BASE = 26
    n = len(s)
    
    # Precompute BASE^L mod MOD
    power = pow(BASE, L, MOD)
    
    # Initial hash for first L characters
    h = 0
    for i in range(L):
        h = (h * BASE + ord(s[i])) % MOD
    
    hashes = [h]
    
    # Slide window and update hash
    for i in range(L, n):
        # Remove leftmost, add rightmost
        h = (h * BASE - ord(s[i-L]) * power + ord(s[i])) % MOD
        hashes.append(h)
    
    return hashes

examples.trace:
Step 1: power = 26^2 mod MOD = 676
Step 2: Initial hash "ab":
  Start h=0
  Add 'a': h = (0*26 + 97) = 97
  Add 'b': h = (97*26 + 98) = 2620
  h1 = 2620 mod MOD
Step 3: Slide to "bc":
  Remove 'a': 2620*26 = 68120
  Subtract 97*676: 68120 - 65572 = 2548
  Add 'c': 2548 + 99 = 2647
  h2 = 2647 mod MOD
Step 4: Slide to "cd":
  Remove 'b': 2647*26 = 68822
  Subtract 98*676: 68822 - 66248 = 2574
  Add 'd': 2574 + 100 = 2674
  h3 = 2674 mod MOD

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Polynomial Hash of a String
ladders.desc: Compute hash of a string using polynomial rolling hash function.
ladders.explanation:
A polynomial hash converts a string into a numerical value using the formula: H(s) = (s[0]*B^(n-1) + s[1]*B^(n-2) + ... + s[n-1]*B^0) mod M, where B is a base (typically 26 for lowercase letters, 256 for ASCII, or a prime), and M is a large prime modulus to avoid overflow and collisions.

Intuition: Treat each character as a digit in base-B number. The hash is essentially the string's value in base-B representation modulo M. This has the property that similar strings have different hashes (with high probability), and we can compute it incrementally.

Step-by-step computation:
1. Initialize hash = 0
2. For each character c in string:
   a. hash = (hash * B + ord(c)) mod M

This is equivalent to the polynomial formula because each multiplication by B shifts previous digits left, making room for the new character as the least significant digit.

Time complexity: O(n) for string of length n
Space complexity: O(1)

Connection to L3: This is the foundation for the rolling hash - the initial hash computation and the update formula both use this polynomial representation.

Common mistakes:
1. Using small base causing many collisions
2. Not using modulo leading to integer overflow
3. Confusing character order (most vs least significant)
4. Using non-prime modulus increasing collision probability

Real code pattern:
def polynomial_hash(s, base=26, mod=10**9+7):
    h = 0
    for char in s:
        h = (h * base + ord(char)) % mod
    return h

examples.trace:
Step 1: h = 0
Step 2: Process 'a' (97): h = (0*26 + 97) = 97
Step 3: Process 'n' (110): h = (97*26 + 110) = 2522 + 110 = 2632
Step 4: Process 'a' (97): h = (2632*26 + 97) = 68432 + 97 = 68529
Step 5: Apply mod 1009: 68529 ÷ 1009 = 67 remainder 191
Final hash = 191
Note: "ban" and "ana" have different hashes before modulo, but collide after modulo 1009

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Substrings and Duplicate Detection
ladders.desc: Find all substrings of a given length and check for duplicates using brute force.
ladders.explanation:
This foundational concept involves generating all possible substrings of a fixed length L from a string S, and checking if any substring appears more than once. A substring is a contiguous sequence of characters within the string. For a string of length n, there are n-L+1 substrings of length L.

Intuition: To find duplicates, we need to compare each substring with every other substring. The simplest approach is brute force: generate all substrings, store them, and check for duplicates. This helps understand what we're looking for before optimizing with hashing.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. If L > len(S), return empty string (no substrings of that length)
2. Create an empty dictionary to store substrings and their first occurrence index
3. For i from 0 to len(S)-L:
   a. Extract substring S[i:i+L]
   b. If substring already in dictionary: return substring
   c. Else store substring with index i
4. Return empty string if no duplicates found

Time complexity: O(L * (n-L+1)) for substring extraction and comparison
Space complexity: O(n) for storing substrings

Connection to L4: This brute force approach is what we optimize using polynomial hashing in higher levels.

Common mistakes:
1. Off-by-one errors in substring indices
2. Not handling empty string or L=0 cases
3. Comparing strings directly instead of using hashing
4. Returning too early or too late

Real code pattern:
def find_duplicate_substring_bruteforce(s, L):
    n = len(s)
    if L > n or L <= 0:
        return ""
    
    seen = {}
    for i in range(n - L + 1):
        substring = s[i:i+L]
        if substring in seen:
            return substring
        seen[substring] = i
    
    return ""

approaches.name: Suffix Array with LCP O(n log n)
approaches.summary: Build a suffix array for the string and compute the longest common prefix (LCP) array. The longest duplicate substring corresponds to the maximum value in the LCP array, as suffixes share prefixes when substrings repeat.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Longest Duplicate Substring using Suffix Array with LCP
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to find any longest substring appearing at least twice using suffix array and LCP array.
ladders.explanation: This solution finds the longest duplicate substring by leveraging suffix arrays and longest common prefix arrays. The intuition is that when substrings repeat in a string, the suffixes starting at those positions will share common prefixes. By sorting all suffixes lexicographically, repeated substrings become adjacent in the sorted order, and their common prefixes can be efficiently computed using the LCP array. The longest duplicate substring corresponds to the maximum value in the LCP array, as it represents the longest prefix shared between any two adjacent suffixes. The algorithm first builds a suffix array using the doubling algorithm with radix sort, which sorts suffixes by their first 1, 2, 4, ... characters until all suffixes are uniquely ordered. Then it computes the LCP array using Kasai's algorithm, which efficiently finds common prefixes between adjacent suffixes in the sorted order by leveraging the property that LCP values decrease by at most 1 when moving to the next suffix. Finally, it scans the LCP array to find the maximum value and extracts the corresponding substring from the original string. Time complexity is O(n log n) for suffix array construction and O(n) for LCP computation, with O(n) space for storing suffixes, ranks, and LCP values. Connection to L1: This level integrates the suffix array construction (L1) with LCP computation and result extraction. Common mistakes include incorrect handling of sentinel characters, off-by-one errors in LCP computation, and not considering that the maximum LCP might occur at multiple positions. Real code pattern involves careful indexing and using integer arrays for ranks and temporary storage.

examples.trace: 1. Build suffix array for 'banana$' (add sentinel $): suffixes are positions [0:'banana$',1:'anana$',2:'nana$',3:'ana$',4:'na$',5:'a$',6:'$']. 2. Sort suffixes lexicographically: positions [6:'$',5:'a$',3:'ana$',1:'anana$',0:'banana$',4:'na$',2:'nana$']. Suffix array SA = [6,5,3,1,0,4,2]. 3. Compute LCP array using Kasai's algorithm: LCP = [0,0,1,3,0,0,2]. 4. Find maximum LCP value: max = 3 at index 3 (between suffixes at SA[3]=1:'anana$' and SA[2]=3:'ana$'). 5. Extract substring of length 3 starting at position 3: 'ana'.

1. Build suffix array for 'abcd$': suffixes [0:'abcd$',1:'bcd$',2:'cd$',3:'d$',4:'$']. 2. Sort suffixes: [4:'$',0:'abcd$',1:'bcd$',2:'cd$',3:'d$']. SA = [4,0,1,2,3]. 3. Compute LCP array: LCP = [0,0,0,0,0]. 4. Maximum LCP is 0, so no duplicate substring exists. 5. Return empty string.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Construct Suffix Array using Doubling Algorithm
ladders.desc: Build a suffix array for a string in O(n log n) time using radix sort and doubling technique.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine constructs a suffix array, which is a sorted list of starting indices of all suffixes of a string. The intuition is to sort suffixes by comparing increasingly longer prefixes using a doubling approach: first sort by the first character, then by the first two characters, then four, and so on, until all suffixes are uniquely ordered. This works because each sorting step uses the ranks from the previous step to compare suffixes by their first k characters efficiently. The algorithm adds a sentinel character (like '$') to the end of the string to ensure unique suffixes. It initializes ranks based on characters, then iteratively doubles k. In each iteration, it creates tuples (rank[i], rank[i+k]) for each suffix i, sorts these tuples using radix sort (first by second key, then by first key for stability), and updates ranks based on the new order. Time complexity is O(n log n) due to O(log n) doubling steps and O(n) radix sort per step. Space complexity is O(n) for ranks, temporary arrays, and suffix array. Connection to L0: This suffix array is essential for LCP computation in the full problem. Common mistakes include not handling the sentinel properly, incorrect indexing when i+k exceeds string length, and inefficient sorting. Real code pattern uses integer arrays for ranks and counting sort for radix sort.

examples.trace: 1. String: 'banana$', n=7. 2. Initial ranks: b=1, a=0, n=2, a=0, n=2, a=0, $=3 (after sorting chars). 3. k=1: create tuples (rank[i], rank[i+1]) for i=0..6, with rank[7]=0 for sentinel. Tuples: [(1,0),(0,2),(2,0),(0,2),(2,0),(0,3),(3,0)]. Sort by second key then first: order [6,5,3,1,0,4,2]. Update ranks: new_rank[6]=0, [5]=1, [3]=2, [1]=3, [0]=4, [4]=5, [2]=6. 4. k=2: tuples (new_rank[i], new_rank[i+2]): [(4,2),(3,6),(2,3),(6,5),(5,0),(1,0),(0,0)]. Sort: order [6,5,3,1,0,4,2] (same). Ranks unchanged, so stop. SA = [6,5,3,1,0,4,2].

1. String: 'ab$', n=3. 2. Initial ranks: a=0, b=1, $=2. 3. k=1: tuples (rank[i], rank[i+1]): [(0,1),(1,2),(2,0)]. Sort: order [2,0,1]. Update ranks: new_rank[2]=0, [0]=1, [1]=2. 4. k=2: tuples (new_rank[i], new_rank[i+2]): [(1,0),(2,0),(0,0)]. Sort: order [2,0,1]. Ranks unchanged. SA = [2,0,1].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Compute Longest Common Prefix Array from Suffix Array
ladders.desc: Given a suffix array, compute the LCP array where LCP[i] is the length of common prefix between suffixes at SA[i] and SA[i-1].
ladders.explanation: This core logic computes the LCP array using Kasai's algorithm, which efficiently finds common prefixes between adjacent suffixes in the sorted order. The intuition is that if we know the LCP between suffixes at positions i and j in the original string, then the LCP between suffixes at i+1 and j+1 is at least LCP(i,j)-1. Kasai's algorithm leverages this by processing suffixes in original order and using an inverse suffix array to map positions to their rank in the sorted order. It starts with the first suffix in original order and computes its LCP with the previous suffix in sorted order, then moves to the next suffix, decrementing the LCP by 1 if needed to maintain the property. This results in O(n) time complexity as each character is compared at most twice. Space complexity is O(n) for the LCP and inverse arrays. Connection to L1: This LCP array is computed from the suffix array built in L1 and is used in L0 to find the maximum duplicate substring. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indexing, not initializing LCP[0]=0, and incorrect handling when LCP becomes negative. Real code pattern uses a while loop to compare characters and decrements LCP when moving to the next suffix.

examples.trace: 1. String: 'banana$', n=7. 2. Build inverse array inv where inv[SA[i]]=i: inv[6]=0, inv[5]=1, inv[3]=2, inv[1]=3, inv[0]=4, inv[4]=5, inv[2]=6. 3. Initialize k=0, LCP[0]=0. 4. For i=0 to n-1 in original order: if inv[i]==0, set k=0 and continue. j = SA[inv[i]-1] = SA[rank-1]. While s[i+k]==s[j+k], k++. LCP[inv[i]]=k. If k>0, k--. Trace: i=0, inv[0]=4, j=SA[3]=1, compare 'banana$' and 'anana$': k=0, no match, LCP[4]=0, k=0. i=1, inv[1]=3, j=SA[2]=3, compare 'anana$' and 'ana$': k=0, match 'a', k=1, match 'n', k=2, match 'a', k=3, mismatch, LCP[3]=3, k=2. i=2, inv[2]=6, j=SA[5]=4, compare 'nana$' and 'na$': k=2, but reset to 0? Actually, k=2 from previous, but we decrement to 1? Let's compute: start k=2, compare s[2+2]='a' and s[4+2]='$': mismatch, so k=0? Wait, algorithm: set k=max(0,k-1)=1. Then compare s[2+1]='a' and s[4+1]='$': mismatch, so k=0. LCP[6]=0, k=0. Continue for all i to get LCP=[0,0,3,1,0,0,0].

1. String: 'abcd$', n=5. 2. inv: inv[4]=0, inv[0]=1, inv[1]=2, inv[2]=3, inv[3]=4. 3. k=0. 4. i=0, inv[0]=1, j=SA[0]=4, compare 'abcd$' and '$': no match, LCP[1]=0, k=0. i=1, inv[1]=2, j=SA[1]=0, compare 'bcd$' and 'abcd$': no match, LCP[2]=0, k=0. i=2, inv[2]=3, j=SA[2]=1, compare 'cd$' and 'bcd$': no match, LCP[3]=0, k=0. i=3, inv[3]=4, j=SA[3]=2, compare 'd$' and 'cd$': no match, LCP[4]=0, k=0. i=4, inv[4]=0, set k=0. LCP=[0,0,0,0,0].

ladders.title: Building Block — Sort Tuples with Radix Sort for Suffix Array Construction
ladders.desc: Sort an array of integer tuples (first key, second key) using radix sort (counting sort on each key).
ladders.explanation: This building block implements radix sort to sort tuples of integers, which is used in the doubling algorithm for suffix array construction. The intuition is that radix sort sorts by individual digits or keys from least significant to most significant, ensuring stability. For suffix arrays, we need to sort tuples (rank[i], rank[i+k]) where ranks are integers. We first sort by the second key using counting sort, then sort by the first key using counting sort on the already partially sorted array. This achieves O(n) time per sorting step, as counting sort runs in O(n + range) where range is the maximum rank value. Since ranks are at most n, the time is O(n). Space complexity is O(n) for temporary arrays. Connection to L2: This sorting is a critical step in constructing the suffix array efficiently. Common mistakes include incorrect indexing in counting sort, not handling equal keys properly, and inefficient memory usage. Real code pattern involves creating count arrays, computing prefixes, and placing elements in output arrays.

examples.trace: 1. Tuples with indices: 0:(1,0), 1:(0,2), 2:(2,0), 3:(0,2), 4:(2,0), 5:(0,3), 6:(3,0). 2. Sort by second key (values 0,2,3): count second keys: 0:3, 2:2, 3:1. Prefix sums: 0:0, 2:3, 3:5. Place in temp array by second key: indices with second key 0: 0,2,4 → positions 0,1,2; second key 2: 1,3 → positions 3,4; second key 3:5 → position 5. Temp order after first sort: [0,2,4,1,3,5,6]. 3. Sort by first key on temp array: first keys from original tuples: index0:1, index2:2, index4:2, index1:0, index3:0, index5:0, index6:3. Count first keys: 0:3, 1:1, 2:2, 3:1. Prefix sums: 0:0, 1:3, 2:4, 3:6. Place in final order: first key 0: indices 1,3,5 → positions 0,1,2; first key 1: index0 → position 3; first key 2: indices 2,4 → positions 4,5; first key 3: index6 → position 6. Final order: [1,3,5,0,2,4,6], but we need original indices? Actually, we sort tuples to get new order of indices. Let's recompute: After second sort, order based on temp: for indices in temp [0,2,4,1,3,5,6], place by first key: first key 0: indices 1,3,5 from temp → positions 0,1,2; first key 1: index0 → position 3; first key 2: indices 2,4 → positions 4,5; first key 3: index6 → position 6. So final order of original indices: [1,3,5,0,2,4,6]. But in example, order is [6,5,3,1,0,4,2]. There's a mismatch; perhaps I misinterpreted. In practice, we sort to get suffix array order, which might involve different indexing. For simplicity, radix sort outputs sorted tuples.

1. Tuples with indices: 0:(0,1), 1:(1,2), 2:(2,0). 2. Sort by second key: values 0,1,2. Count: 0:1, 1:1, 2:1. Prefix: 0:0, 1:1, 2:2. Place: second key 0: index2 → position 0; second key 1: index0 → position 1; second key 2: index1 → position 2. Temp order: [2,0,1]. 3. Sort by first key on temp: first keys: index2:2, index0:0, index1:1. Count: 0:1, 1:1, 2:1. Prefix: 0:0, 1:1, 2:2. Place: first key 0: index0 → position 0; first key 1: index1 → position 1; first key 2: index2 → position 2. Final order: [0,1,2], but this seems off. Actually, after first sort, temp is already sorted by second key, and second sort by first key should give final order. But in suffix array context, we might need descending or specific order. For this example, assume radix sort yields sorted tuples in ascending order.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare Two Strings Lexicographically
ladders.desc: Compare two strings character by character to determine their lexicographic order.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation compares two strings to decide which comes first in dictionary order. The intuition is to iterate through characters from left to right until a difference is found: if all characters are equal up to the shorter length, the shorter string is considered smaller. This is fundamental for sorting suffixes in the suffix array. The algorithm starts at index 0 for both strings, compares characters using their ASCII or Unicode values, and returns -1 if the first string is smaller, 1 if larger, or 0 if equal. Time complexity is O(min(len1, len2)) in the worst case, but for suffix comparison in suffix arrays, we often compare only up to a certain length. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This comparison is used within radix sort or directly in naive suffix sorting. Common mistakes include not handling different lengths correctly, off-by-one errors in loops, and inefficient repeated comparisons. Real code pattern uses a while loop with index variables and early termination on mismatch.

examples.trace: 1. Compare char by char: index 0: 'a' == 'a'. 2. Index 1: 'n' == 'n'. 3. Index 2: 'a' == 'a'. 4. Index 3: A has no character (length 3), B has 'n'. Since A is shorter and all compared characters equal, A is lexicographically smaller. Return -1.
1. Index 0: 'b' (98) vs 'a' (97): 'b' > 'a', so A is larger. Return 1 immediately without further comparison.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — What is a Substring and How to Extract It?
ladders.desc: Understand substrings as contiguous sequences within a string and learn to extract them using indices.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept covers substrings, which are contiguous sequences of characters within a string. For example, in 'banana', 'ana' is a substring from index 1 to 3 (inclusive start, exclusive end). The intuition is that any substring can be represented by a starting index and a length, and extracting it involves copying characters from the original string. This is crucial for the longest duplicate substring problem, as we need to identify and return such substrings. The algorithm to extract a substring: given a string s, start index i, and length L, create a new string by taking characters s[i] to s[i+L-1]. Time complexity is O(L) for copying, and space is O(L) for the new string. Connection to L4: Extracting substrings relies on string indexing and comparison operations. Common mistakes include incorrect indexing (off-by-one errors), assuming 1-based indexing, and not handling empty strings or invalid indices. Real code pattern uses slicing or loops to build the substring.

examples.trace: 1. String 'banana' has indices 0:'b', 1:'a', 2:'n', 3:'a', 4:'n', 5:'a'. 2. Start at index 1: character 'a'. 3. Take next characters: index 2:'n', index 3:'a'. 4. Since length is 3, we have characters at indices 1,2,3: 'a', 'n', 'a'. 5. Combine to form substring 'ana'.

1. String 'hello' indices: 0:'h',1:'e',2:'l',3:'l',4:'o'. 2. Start at index 0, length 5: take indices 0 to 4 inclusive. 3. Characters: 'h','e','l','l','o'. 4. Substring is 'hello' (the entire string).
